notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your transactions show $1,650 at Bright Horizons Daycare and $220 at Care.com Backup Care in May — that's $1,870 in qualifying childcare expenses last month alone. Since tax matters involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Here's how the two main federal tax breaks work, and how they likely apply to your situation.
The two federal childcare tax breaks
Child and Dependent Care Credit (CDCTC)
This is a direct reduction in your tax bill — not just a deduction. You can count up to $6,000 in qualifying expenses for two or more children (or $3,000 for one child). Based on your household income, which appears to be around $216,450 in combined gross income (from your two paystubs), you'd likely fall in the 20% credit range on those expenses.
At 20%, that translates to a potential credit of up to $1,200 for one child's qualifying expenses ($6,000 cap × 20%) or up to $600 per child at lower expense caps. The credit is non-refundable, meaning it reduces taxes owed but doesn't generate a refund if it exceeds your liability.
Dependent Care FSA (DCAP)
This lets you set aside pre-tax dollars through your employer to pay for childcare. The 2026 limit just increased to $7,500 per household (up from $5,000 previously). If you're in roughly the 22–24% federal bracket based on your combined income, sheltering $7,500 pre-tax could save you approximately $1,650–$1,800 in federal income tax alone — plus FICA savings on top of that.
The coordination rule you need to know
You cannot double-count the same expenses for both benefits. If you contribute $7,500 to a Dependent Care FSA, that reduces your CDCTC-eligible expenses dollar-for-dollar. In most cases for households at your income level, maximizing the FSA first tends to provide more value because it reduces both income tax and FICA taxes, whereas the credit only reduces income tax.
The practical priority order for most families in your situation:
Step 1: Maximize the Dependent Care FSA ($7,500) through whichever employer offers it — check both your Salesforce and UCHealth benefit elections
Step 2: Claim the CDCTC on Form 2441 for any qualifying expenses above the FSA amount (up to the $6,000 cap, reduced by FSA contributions)
What qualifies
Your Bright Horizons Daycare and Care.com Backup Care expenses both appear to qualify — they're exactly the type of licensed care and backup childcare the IRS recognizes. Expenses must be for care that enables you to work, and the child must be under 13.
To claim the credit at tax time, you'll need each provider's name, address, and Tax ID number (EIN for a daycare center, SSN for an individual caregiver). Bright Horizons should provide this on a year-end statement.
Do you know whether either of your employers currently offers a Dependent Care FSA, and if so, whether you're enrolled?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
